serving.natural_language_portion: A typical culinary serving of caraway seeds is approximately 1 teaspoon (about 2.1 g), used as a spice in bread, stews, or vegetable dishes.

nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for raw caraway seeds used as primary nutrient reference. HPB Singapore values from input record are consistent with USDA data and are retained where aligned. USDA values used for saturated fat and additional micronutrients not present in HPB record.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Spices and Seeds — analogous estimate based on high-fibre seed category; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for caraway seeds exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate based on the high dietary fibre content (38 g/100g) and low sugar content (0.64 g/100g) of caraway seeds, consistent with other high-fibre seeds and spices in the low-GI category (GI < 55). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (40 × 49.9 × 0.021) / 100 ≈ 0.4. Used in small culinary quantities; actual glycemic impact per serving is negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Exceptionally low sodium (17 mg/100g) and very high potassium (1351 mg/100g) yielding an outstanding potassium-to-sodium ratio of approximately 79:1, supporting DASH dietary principles.
hypertension.guidance: Caraway seeds are highly suitable for individuals with hypertension; use freely as a flavouring agent in place of salt, noting that culinary serving sizes of 1–5 g mean absolute potassium intake per serving is modest but cumulative benefit is meaningful across a spice-rich diet.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Exceptionally high dietary fibre (38 g/100g) slows gastric emptying and glucose absorption; negligible sugar (0.64 g/100g); bioactive compounds carvone and limonene have demonstrated hypoglycaemic properties in preclinical studies.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Caraway seeds are appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as a culinary spice; their high fibre content and low sugar contribute to a low estimated glycemic index (~40), and regular inclusion in a spice-rich diet may provide cumulative benefit to postprandial glucose control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.62 g/100g), zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre (38 g/100g), and high potassium (1351 mg/100g) collectively support cardiovascular health; flavonoid polyphenols in caraway seeds have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Caraway seeds are cardioprotective as a culinary spice; their negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and high fibre content align with heart-healthy dietary guidelines, and their polyphenol content may contribute to reduced oxidative stress when consumed regularly as part of a balanced diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory bioactives (carvone, limonene, flavonoid polyphenols) and high fibre supporting gut microbiome health relevant to immune modulation in RA.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Caraway seeds may be used freely by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as a culinary spice; their anti-inflammatory phytochemical content and gut-supportive fibre may provide modest adjunct benefit, though serving sizes are small and should not replace prescribed RA management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Caraway seed (Carum carvi) is a dried aromatic spice consisting of small, dark brown, crescent-shaped seeds with a firm texture and a distinctive earthy, anise-like flavour profile derived from its primary volatile compounds carvone and limonene. Native to Europe and Western Asia, caraway seeds have been used for millennia in European, Middle Eastern, and North African cuisines as a flavouring for rye bread, sauerkraut, stews, cheeses, and liqueurs such as aquavit and kümmel. The seeds are typically used whole or lightly crushed and are not cooked as a standalone food but rather as a culinary spice in small quantities of 1–5 g per serving. Nutritionally, caraway seeds are exceptionally rich in dietary fibre (38 g/100g), protein (19.77 g/100g), and minerals including calcium (689 mg/100g), potassium (1351 mg/100g), and iron (16.23 mg/100g), though these values are per 100 g and actual intake per culinary serving is modest. The seeds are low in sodium (17 mg/100g) and contain negligible sugar (0.64 g/100g), making them suitable for hypertension and Type 2 Diabetes management as a dietary adjunct. Their bioactive phytochemicals — particularly carvone and limonene — have demonstrated anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and hypoglycaemic properties in preclinical research. Caraway seeds are plant-based, gluten-free, halal, kosher, and suitable for vegan and vegetarian diets. They are classified as a minimally processed raw dried spice.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170921) for raw caraway seeds — an exact match to the food identity. HPB Singapore input values are consistent with USDA data. Additional micronutrients populated from USDA. GI is an analogous estimate due to absence of direct measurement in the University of Sydney GI Database, which is standard for spices used in small culinary quantities.
notes: 1. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg/100g — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; caraway seeds are a plant-based food and inherently contain no cholesterol. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 mcg/100g — confirmed zero per USDA; plant-based food with no B12. Vitamin D is 0.0 mcg/100g — confirmed zero per USDA for raw dried caraway seeds. Trans fat is 0.0 g/100g — confirmed zero per USDA. 2. Saturated fat (0.62 g/100g), monounsaturated fat (7.93 g/100g), polyunsaturated fat (3.27 g/100g), and all micronutrient values not present in the HPB input record are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 170921. 3. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for caraway seeds exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The estimate is based on the food's exceptionally high dietary fibre content (38 g/100g) and negligible sugar, consistent with low-GI high-fibre seeds. Glycemic load per serving (2.1 g) is approximately 0.4, which is nutritionally negligible. 4. Ontology codes: FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03301710 and SNOMED CT code 412068001 are cited as defensible matches; reviewers should verify against current ontology releases. FoodEx2 code A00LC is cited as a defensible match for dried caraway seeds in the EFSA FoodEx2 system. 5. No mandatory multilingual names are required for this food as it is not classified as a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; it is a globally used spice of European/Western Asian origin. 6. The 'wholegrain' dietary tag from the input record has been removed as caraway seeds are not a grain product; retained tags are plant_based, low_sodium, high_protein, and high_fibre.
